# FAQs

How do I know which TENA MEN product to use? If you're unsure about which product to try first I recommend the Keep Control sample pack from TENA. It's a pack with free samples from the TENA Men range so you have the option of trying a selection of the products at home before you make your purchase. It's also good to ask your doctor for advice as he or she may have some suggestions about which product might work best for you.

Which is the most absorbent guard for men? Our most absorbent guard is our TENA MEN Super Guard. This guard is meant to help control most leaks experienced by men and it's for day and night use. If you find you need more absorbency, you may want to try the TENA Men’s Underwear for additional protection.

How can I tell the front from the back for TENA MEN guards? For TENA MEN guards you place the wider part of the product is placed in the front of your underwear.

How do you use TENA Men guards/shields? TENA Men Guards feature a strong adhesive strip and a cup shaped design for protection when active. Each guard/shield comes individually wrapped for discreet portability. To use, peel off the paper strip and make sure the wider end is up and the sticky side down before placing within your underwear.

How Can I use my FSA on my TENA Products? It's easy! Some FSA programs let you use an FSA debit card at checkout in store to pay for eligible expenses like TENA liners, pads, underwear and briefs. Other programs require you to submit their employer specific reimbursement form. For these purchases and when you buy directly from our website, TENA.us, pay for your products, keep the receipt and then complete & submit a reimbursement form. The reimbursement form, is usually available when you login into your online FSA account,. Detailed instructions are usually included with the form. Your program will process your request promptly and reimburse you either through direct deposit into your bank account or send you a check.

##### best comfortable surge protection

I have been wearing mens pads for surge protection for almost 2 years. Most pads are too narrow to catch the occasional surge, and worse, they have a tendency to fold in two length-wise, further reducing their usefulness. These TENA Incontinence Guards for Men, Moderate Absorbency are the best thing I have found for the protection from leaks. They have a shallow cup shape that follows the anatomy they are covering, and they are lightweight. I forget I have the pads in place, they are that comfortable. They are shorter than the typical pad, but triangle in shape and protect more of the area that needs the coverage. In the second photo on the image on the left I've placed the TENA pad over the pad I have been using to show a size comparison, and on the image on the right I placed the previous pad on top to show the better coverage of the TENA pad.

These are ideal for men perhaps several months after surgery, when your leakage is low, as they are less bulky and obviously absorb less liguid than TENA's heavier grade pads, which were very useful right after surgery, when you might have leaked a cup of urine a day.







\*\*\*Practicing Kegels before surgery, then doing you Kegels often each day, starting a week or so after the surgery, REALLY helps with a quicker recovery!!







When you heal and recover to where you only have one to a few unexpected small "spurts" a day, then this thinner TENA pad could be ideal for you.







TENA's in general are very absorbent compared to other brands I've tried, comparing same thickness, and they hide any odor of urine well for hours. The have a better "wicking' material, that's why.



The slightly higher price for them, compared to the cheaper pads sold at "big box" stores is well worth the difference, as a thinner TENA is a lot more comfortable than a less tailored, less aborbent, and bulkier cheaper brand.







The fit is much better for me than, say, \*Depends, and that brand difference totally depends on just how a particular pad conforms to your body/underwear. Some folks prefer the \*Depends fit.



Price matters more for some folks, too.



Your mileage may vary.



Try different brands and see. I bought some pads from different brands, that happened to be donated to a Goodwill store, to compare them, and not waste much money doing so.







By the time 6-12 months pass after surgery, most men "should" have little need of a pad. Even with the same surgeon, the time until you are







'"dry" every day will vary by months for their patients.



Shop around for surgeons, overall, some surgeons just have better technique than others.



Get an "A" surgeon, and not a 'C" grade surgeon!







Some days I'm "dry" and can wear the dry pad for 2-3 days before it gets wet. That's when a pad's adhesive is really tested!



I'm also almost a year past surgery, so I hope to not "need" any pads in the future, yet I'll still have one on, "just in case" if I'm in public, and can't get to a bathroom for hours-as when your more "full" bladder is most likely to sneak out a "spurt".



Solution-don't drink a lot of fluids for 2 hours before an event, and pee just when you get to an event, even when you don't feel like you need to!







I'm 5'10' and 220 lbs, and the pad is too wide at the top and rubs on me, so I cut it roughly in half, horizontally and use the smaller, narrower bottom half only, and that is a better fit and feel for me through the day-the rubbing on the side and top used to drive me crazy when I walked.



Maybe the whole pad is the right size/ fit for you.







I learned that talking to other men after surgery, and looking on forums, that their daily urine leakage lessened as their body learned to "hold it in" especially while laughing, or suddenly standing up. (Go Kegels!)



Fortunately , their need for pad size and thickness definitely changed to "smaller and thinner" with time, as things improved.







Cons: The adhesive strip is at best mediocre. They've got to up their game on making this "stickier." One star off.

Ok for someone who never writes reviews I felt like I needed to this time. Long story short I had prostate cancer in 2012 and have had bladder issues after radiation ever since. Not bad but needed a fix as anyone that has an issue will tell you. The first thing I tried was depends underwear. They worked but overkill , expensive, and for me and was a little embarrassing around my girlfriend. I then tried depends pads and they worked but they were bulky and agin maybe overkill and they leaked out the sides. Then switched to depends thin pads, easy to pack on trips but they leaked out the sides and I felt like I needed just a little more maybe somewhere in the middle between the light and heavier pads but with some of leak protection on the sides. Depends had no options here. Thats when I discovered these. Perfect pad size, small flaps on the side so there is no side leakage and they keep me dry all day. I would give these for my situation 5 stars! They are the perfect all around protection without going overboard and they are also easy to pack on trips. Will continue with these absolutely! You can carry extra ones on those days if needed in case of accidents.
